entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Upgrade (Version 2026.2.6 – QRC 1/2026)
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We have recently upgraded our SAP HANA Cloud system to version 2026.2.6 (QRC 1/2026).

Post-upgrade, we are observing multiple behavioral changes in the system, including:

  • Increased frequency of column store unloads (Reason: MERGE)
  • Changes in delta merge behavior
  • Performance variations during application testing

We would like to understand the changes introduced in this release.

Requesting your support to provide:

  1. Official release notes / documentation for SAP HANA Cloud version 2026.2.6 (QRC 1/2026)
  2. Details of changes related to:
    • Delta merge behavior
    • Memory management and unload mechanisms
    • Query execution / optimizer changes
  3. Any known issues or limitations identified in this release
  4. Recommended best practices or configurations post-upgrade
  5. Any parameter changes or default configuration updates

entry.title: Automating SAP CPI Deployment Validation : IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts API in Real-Time Monitoring
entry.content:
Background & Problem Statement

As an integration developer we know that our SAP Integration Suite landscape handles hundreds of integration flows across multiple environments — Dev, QA, Pre-Prod/Production.

In many release cycles, our team faces a few pain points like:

Deployed iFlow → Assumed it started → Found error hours later
Wrong version deployed → No one noticed until message failures
Manual checks → Time-consuming → Human error prone
No centralized visibility → Each team checking independently

We can have an automated, programmatic way to:

  1. Verify deployment success immediately after release
  2. Check runtime status in real-time
  3. Integrate this check into our CI/CD pipeline

Discovering the API

SAP Integration Suite exposes a rich set of OData APIs for managing and monitoring integration artifacts.

The key API I used:

GET /api/v1/IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts('{iflowID}')

what this API returns:

  <properties>
<Id>jmsChecksdummy2</Id>
        <Version>1.0.0</Version>
        <Name>jmsChecksdummy2</Name>
        <Type>INTEGRATION_FLOW</Type>
        <DeployedBy>Rohit.Parihar@syngenta.com</DeployedBy>
        <DeployedOn>2026-02-25T09:46:43.180</DeployedOn>
        <Status>STARTED</Status>
    </properties>
 
Design: I understand that the CPI developer does not need steps on how to create the flow and utilize the objects however, I am pasting the flow skeleton here for reference.
  • I've used two integration processes, both of which can run independently. One runs on a timer-based schedule and the other through an external system using a REST endpoint.
  • The body remains the same but can be dynamically modified to fetch information for multiple artifacts at once. Since it is externalized, it can be configured without editing the flow.
  • The payload can also be logged in the message processing logs. This is condition-based, meaning if the Log Payload flag as header is set to true, the payload will be logged otherwise, it will not.
 One of the example of the input I'm passing and the response is pasted below.
<root>
<iflowID>jmsChecksDummy</iflowID>
<iflowID>jmsChecks</iflowID>
</root>

 

 

Conclusion

The information is helpful for identifying versions mismatch, changes and stage of runtime artifacts in real time.

Note: designtime artifacts information will not include status information.

Hope this helps someone someday, happy learning!

entry.title: Issue Updating IS-Oil Fields (UOM Group & Conversion Group) via FM MATERIAL_UPDATE_ALL
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am extending a material to multiple plants and need to update IS-Oil specific fields in the material master, namely UOM Group and Conversion Group (IS-Oil view). For this, I am using the function module MATERIAL_UPDATE_ALL. Although the FM executes successfully without any errors and updates standard fields correctly, the IS-Oil fields are not getting updated. The material is extended to the plant properly, required values are passed in the relevant structures, and debugging does not show any explicit error—yet the changes are not reflected in the material master.

Could you please confirm whether MATERIAL_UPDATE_ALL supports updating IS-Oil fields like UOM Group and Conversion Group? Are there any specific structures (e.g., OIB* tables), update indicators, or additional flags required to update these fields? Alternatively, is there any recommended BAPI/FM for maintaining IS-Oil data in the material master? Any guidance would be highly appreciated.

Thanks & Regards,

Neeraj Jain

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-04-06
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As we transition into the second quarter of the year, it’s the perfect moment to showcase the updates and enhancements introduced during February and March 2026. This edition of What’s New provides an overview of the latest features and improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM).

If you’d like to revisit previous updates, feel free to explore posts under the PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag.

With that, let’s dive into what’s new for February and March.

 

Enhanced Authorization Control for Runtime Artifacts

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) now offers enhanced authorization control for runtime artifacts, ensuring that users can access only the information relevant to their assigned roles.

With this enhancement, access permissions are applied consistently to runtime artifacts. This means that what users can see and work with is aligned with their existing authorizations, creating a clear and consistent experience across the application.

This behavior is applied across the following application groups: Model, Process, Reports, and System. Lists, details, and available actions reflect only the objects that users are permitted to access.

Key Highlights

  • Runtime artifacts (e.g., Environments) are displayed based on user authorizations
  • Value help lists display only authorized artifacts, even when no filters are applied
  • Copy and Copy To actions are allowed only when the user has access to the selected target

For example, the user is assigned to a role collection called “Model Viewer” in SAP BTP, which contains the role MODEL_READ and the user attribute READQAMUSER.

 Image 1. Role Collection and roles assigned to the user in SAP BTP

 

Based on this assignment, the user can view only the runtime environments that match their authorization in the SAP PaPM Cloud UM application. Environments that are not relevant to the user’s role or team assignment are not displayed.

Image 2. Only authorized runtime environments are displayed in the list view.

Image 3. Value Help list displays the runtime environments the user is authorized to access.

This enhancement helps ensure controlled access while simplifying the user experience by displaying only relevant information.

For more information about roles, see: Role Templates in Universal Model.

 

Report

Environment Scope Toggle for Joule

This feature activates an intelligent QnA agent (Joule Bot) directly within the SAP PaPM Cloud UM Report Screen.

It empowers users to ask natural language questions about business environments, such as BEPS or Cost Allocation, and receive precise, context-aware answers.

How It Works:

By default, the Environment Scope toggle is switched off. Activate this option if you want to receive answers related to the business environment. 
 
The response behavior depends on the environment’s Runtime Status and the state of the Environment Scope toggle: 

  • Environment Scope: ON
    • Current: Joule provides answers based on the current environment’s runtime functions data. 
    • Inactive: No results are returned. 
  • Environment Scope: OFF
    • Joule provides answers based on the current report pages, regardless of the runtime status.

This setup allows users to easily switch between environment-specific insights and report-focused queries, ensuring relevant answers without unnecessary complexity.

 

Customizable Aggregated Charts with Grouping in Report Copilot

Users can now dynamically create customizable charts at runtime by specifying aggregations and row groupings on the selected grid columns. 

The generated Show Elements are automatically rendered with appropriate visualizations—such as line, bar, area, or pie charts—without requiring manual configuration.

How It Works:

  • As a prerequisite, ensure that a report is created and open it in Edit Mode.
  • The following prompts can be used as examples:
    • “Add a bar chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and max aggregated values”
    • “Add a Pie chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and min aggregated values”
    • “Add an Area chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and sum aggregated values”
    • “Add a Line chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and average aggregated values”

       

System

Enhanced Event Log when writing using SOAP Service 

In the SAP PaPM Cloud UM,  when users run the Writer function to send data to SAP S/4 HANA Cloud using the SOAP Service, an information prompt appears showing the message ID to indicate success. This is used as a reference when validating the writing from Universal Model onto the target platform SAP S/4 HANA Cloud. With this enhancement, the message ID is now displayed once per run of the Writer function using the SOAP Service.

Before

Message ID is shown twice when Writer function using SOAP Service is run.

After

Message ID is shown once when Writer function using SOAP Service is run.

 

 

Data Screen

Color Conditions in Adapt UI

We are introducing Color Conditions in Adapt UI, which is a new feature that enables users to apply dynamic background and/or text colors at the cell level based on configurable conditions.

Color Conditions are configured in Object Page → Adapt UI mode and are automatically reflected on both the Object Page and the Data Screen, ensuring a consistent visual experience across screens. Users can define multiple conditional rules for a field and configure background color, text color, or both. Conditions can also reference the values of other fields within the same row, enabling contextual visual highlighting.

When multiple conditions apply, the first matching rule takes priority. If the first rule defines only one color type (e.g., background only), the system continues evaluating subsequent rules to determine the missing color type and applies the combined result.

The configuration persists after reload and re-login, does not affect exported data, and can be easily removed to restore the default cell styling.  

 

Joule Design Enhancement

The Joule user interface has been enhanced with improved usability and flexibility.

The updated design includes an animated main image to provide a more dynamic interface, with the option to enable or disable animations directly in the Settings menu.

Users can now unlock the Joule window and reposition it anywhere on the screen, allowing for a more flexible workspace layout. In addition, the Chat menu now opens on click, improving interaction clarity and navigation.

An AI Notice has also been added to clearly inform users about AI-powered functionality within Joule.

 

Field Type Icons in Columns Panel 

We’re introducing an enhancement to the Columns Panel that improves how users identify field types.

Icons are now displayed beside each field, allowing users to instantly recognize the type of data they’re working with. This update improves clarity and reduces reliance on field names alone.

 

The following field types are now represented with corresponding icons:

                                         Field Types                                                   Icon

Amount

Association
Association to Many

 

 

 

Composition

 

 

 

Characteristics

 

 

 

In-Data Comment

 

 

Key Figure

 

 

Quantity

 

 

Icons are implemented consistently and behave the same across:

  • Function (Show Data)
  • Activity (Show Data)
  • Report pages (Show Element and Application Element)

This ensures a consistent experience for users whenever they interact with field selections.

Improved visual consistency and clear cues enable users to navigate and interpret data more efficiently, resulting in a smoother overall experience.

 

Preserved Group Expansion State After Sorting

This update improves the behavior of the Data Screen when working with grouped data and sorting. With this enhancement, the system now preserves the expanded and collapsed state of row groups when sorting is applied. Sorting actions now only reorder the data and no longer affect the visibility state of grouped rows.

The improvement applies to all grouping configurations within the Data Screen grid, including groups created via the Column Menu and those defined through the Column Pane (row grouping area). It supports all standard sorting actions such as “Sort Ascending”, “Sort Descending”, and “Clear Sort”, as well as master data sorting options like “Sort by Value”, “Sort by Text”, “Text First”, and “Text Last”. The behavior is consistent for both “Single Column” and “Multiple Column” group display modes.

As a result, users can now sort data without losing their current analytical context. Expanded groups remain expanded, and collapsed groups remain collapsed across all sorting operations, improving usability and efficiency when working with grouped datasets.

Please note that these improvements are also applied in Reports Elements, ensuring consistent behavior across the application.

 

Enhanced Long Text Handling 

This update introduces improved handling of long texts in Data Screen, ensuring a consistent and more readable user experience.

Long text is now fully visible by default, as rows automatically expand to fit the content and prevent truncation of wrapped text.

In Edit Mode, Large String fields are scrollable, allowing users to easily view and edit content without affecting the layout.

Paragraph structure (including line breaks) is preserved for Large String fields and displayed in the original format. Meanwhile, String fields continue to display text without paragraph formatting.

 The Show More / Show Less functionality on the Object Page allows users to easily expand or collapse long text fields.

 

Inline editing has been enhanced to support efficient maintenance of long text directly within the grid. For Large String fields, a pencil icon provides access to a dialog with a text area for more convenient editing. Formatting, including line breaks, is preserved after saving.

 

Additional layout flexibility is introduced through “Compact Rows” and extended column sizing options. Users can choose “Compact Rows” in the Column Menu to switch between fully expanded text (default) and a condensed single-line view, and save this preference as part of a layout.

Please note that these improvements are also applied in Reports Elements, ensuring consistent behavior across the application.

 

That’s a wrap for now!

We hope these new features and enhancements make your experience with Cloud UM even smoother. Keep an eye out for our monthly “What’s New” releases.

Thank you for sticking around! If you found this helpful, feel free to spread the word to others.

entry.title: Серія вебінарів у квітні
entry.content:
Ми зібрали добірку глобальних вебінарів від SAP на квітень — для тих, хто хоче глибше зануритись у хмарну трансформацію, AI, інтеграцію, управління даними та бізнес-процеси.

Від практичних deep-dive сесій до розмов з топ-експертами SAP — обирайте найцікавіші для вас сесії та реєструйтесь за лінками нижче.

⌚Час вказано за Києвом. Мова вебінарів — англійська.

SAP Cloud ERP — трансформація, інновації та AI

  • 7 квітня, 15:00 -16:00
    Understanding SAP Cloud ERP: Public Cloud Concepts &   Operating Model 
    Основи SAP Cloud ERP, її модель операцій в публічній хмарі, клієнтські приклади та модель безпеки.

  • 13 квітня, 10:00 - 11:00
    In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP ERP HY1 - AI Innovations in Supply Chain Management
    Глибоке занурення в AI-можливості SAP Cloud ERP для Supply Chain. Огляд 5 сценаріїв використання: обробка сертифікатів якості (SAP Document AI), управління відставанням замовлень (SAP Joule), агент планування виробництва, створення сповіщень про технічне обслуговування та обробка надходження товарів — з демонстрацією системи та сесією Q&A.

  • 14 квітня, 15:00 -16:00
    Innovation, End to End Scope, and Standard Business Processes
    Огляд ERP, зокрема фінанси, закупівлі та продажі, найкращі E2E бізнес-процеси в SAP Cloud ERP, а також питання конфігурації та стандартизації. Як використання гнучкості допомагає в диференціації та як послідовність процесів може прискорити впровадження.

  • 16 квітня, 12:00 - 12:30
    Clean core. Upgrade ready. Built to innovate. Maximizing value with SAP Cloud ERP Private
    Як clean core стратегія допомагає ERP-системам адаптуватися швидше, впроваджувати ШІ та знижувати витрати на оновлення. Досвід Cimpress та Nippon Gases Europe.

  • 21 квітня, 15:00 -16:00
    Transforming to SAP Cloud ERP: Mindset, Method, and Momentum
    Найкращі практики для трансформаційних проектів, огляд SAP Activate для SAP Cloud ERP у публічній хмарі, аспекти міграції даних та управління змінами у стандартному хмарному середовищі. Поширені помилки та найбільш ефективні підходи.
    Спікер: Sadik Tekin, керівник взаємодії з клієнтами - SAP Cloud ERP.

  • 28 квітня, 15:00 -16:00
    SAP Business AI in SAP Cloud ERP
    Як SAP Business AI інтегровано в SAP Cloud ERP для забезпечення надійних прогнозних інсайтів, інтелектуальної автоматизації та контекстуальних рекомендацій в основних бізнес-процесах. Крім того, будемо розглядати поширені випадки використання AI в SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

SAP Business Data Cloud – Наступна ера бізнес-даних

  • 16 квітня, 17:00 - 18:00
    Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model
    Огляд метрик споживання SAP Business Data Cloud та інструмента SAP BDC Capacity Unit Estimator для обчислення потреб у ємнісних одиницях. Також буде представлена інформація з SAP for Me, яка допоможе зрозуміти, як отримати доступ до важливих даних для підписки та операційних рішень.

SAP BTP: архітектура, AI та управління хмарою

  • 14 квітня, 17:00 - 18:00
    Architect the Future: SAP BTP AI Best Practices: From Strategy to Implementation
    Функціональна та технічна реалізація AI на SAP Business Technology Platform, важливість цих практик, структуру для AI-випадків та релевантні шаблони для проектів. Проєктування, побудова та масштабування AI-рішень на SAP BTP з реальним бізнес-впливом.

  • 15 квітня, 14:30 - 15:30
    Transformation with Agentic Enterprise Automation
    Як SAP BTP та SAP Business Transformation Management разом забезпечують масштабну автоматизацію підприємства на базі ШІ. Дізнайтесь, як проектувати та керувати ШІ-агентами, вимірювати їх цінність і перейти від точкової автоматизації до наскрізної інтелектуальної трансформації процесів — з реальними прикладами.

  • 21 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Govern your cloud deliveries with SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Cloud Transport Management.
    Останні покращення в SAP Cloud ALM та SAP Cloud Transport Management Service. Можливість редагування транспортних запитів у BTP, що дозволяє динамічно налаштовувати транспорти в режимі реального часу. 

  • 23 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Inside SAP IT: How to Lead IT Through Constant Change – a talk with SAP’s CPIO Benni Blau
    Розмова з Dr. Benjamin Blau, Chief Process and Information Officer SAP: як SAP IT управляє одним з найскладніших технологічних ландшафтів у світі, що означає роль Customer Zero та як агентний ШІ змінює роботу IT. Без сценарію. Зсередини.

  • 28 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    SAP Cloud ALM for SAP BTP Operations - Latest news!
    Як SAP Cloud ALM for Operations покращує роботу SAP Business Technology Platform. Огляд та аналіз підтримки SAP BTP. Новини про стратегію та плани на майбутнє. 

Інтеграція

  • 23  квітня, 17:30 - 19:00
    Achieve Enterprise-grade Operations for SAP Integration Suite
    Сесія охоплює операційні моделі, управління та стандартизовані практики експлуатації SAP Integration Suite: централізований моніторинг, варіанти розгортання, автоматизоване управління та DevOps-механізми. Для архітекторів інтеграцій, власників платформ та операційних команд.

  • 28 квітня, 9:30 - 11:00
    Develop enterprise-grade AI Agents with SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite
    Як SAP Build та SAP Integration Suite разом забезпечують наскрізну агентну автоматизацію в SAP та не-SAP середовищах. Дізнайтесь, як поєднати low-code розробку з корпоративною інтеграцією для побудови надійних автоматизацій під управлінням ШІ-агентів. Жива демонстрація та реальні приклади клієнтів включені.

  • 28 квітня, 17:00 - 18:00
    SAP Integration Suite - Monthly Update April
    Дізнайтесь про оновлення SAP Integration Suite — iPaaS платформи для з'єднання хмарних та локальних процесів, застосунків і даних. У програмі: оновлення B2B, нові можливості Edge Integration Cell (файловий адаптер, OFTP2, хмарний конектор) та презентація нової розширеної редакції Enhanced Edition. А також — #AskAnythingIntegration сесія запитань та відповідей з експертами.

Процеси та трансформація з SAP Signavio

  • 23 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Turning business transformation into a capability for competitive advantage: from fragmented initiatives to repeatable success
    Як подолати проблеми масштабування трансформацій та перетворити їх на конкурентну перевагу. Оцінка зрілості процесів: визначте прогалини в стратегії, процесах, даних і культурі за допомогою досвіду 1,000 лідерів. Створіть систему для вимірюваних результатів, швидшого впровадження та інновацій.

Підтримка, сервіс та розвиток

  • 7 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Customer Interaction Center (CIC) Support SAP for Me: SAP for Me Release Updates Q1 2026
    Щоквартальний вебкаст про останні оновлення та нові функції SAP for Me. Огляд змін з попереднього релізу, поточні проєкти та збір відгуків від учасників. 

  • 14 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    SAP's new Service Portfolio - Unleash your full potential with Success Plans
    Як Success Plans допомагають максимізувати цінність ваших SAP-рішень через персоналізовану підтримку та безперервний розвиток.

  • 15 квітня, 11:00 - 12:00
    Global HR, Local Compliance - Unlocking Localization in SAP SuccessFactors
    Як SAP SuccessFactors поєднує стандартизовані глобальні HR-процеси з локальними регуляторними вимогами. Локалізовані версії для розрахунку зарплати, управління часом та core HR, понад 800 законодавчих оновлень на рік. Завершення — жива сесія Q&A.

  • 15 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Unlocking Transformative Value with the Advanced Success Plan
    Дізнайтесь, як Advanced Success Plan допомагає повністю розкрити потенціал ваших SAP-рішень та досягти вимірюваних бізнес-результатів.

  • 16 квітня, 16:00 - 17:00
    Achieving Strategic Excellence with the Max Success Plan
    Стратегічне партнерство та підтримка виділених експертів: наскрізний консалтинг для оцінки нових можливостей, управління складними трансформаціями та досягнення конкурентної переваги.

Переходьте за посиланнями, реєструйтесь та додавайте події до календаря — квітень це чудова можливість прокачати знання, отримати інсайти від експертів та бути в курсі найновіших рішень SAP.

entry.title: Re: SAP Commerce 2211 – Is SMTP OAuth2 (Client Credential...

Hello @stefaniaZ 


kindly find additional information as SAP Commerce Cloud does not provide explicit documentation for OAuth2 SMTP authentication in version 2211. 
Microsoft 365 SMTP OAuth2 guidance: Microsoft Learn - Authenticate an IMAP, POP or SMTP connection using OAuth
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/exchange/client-developer/legacy-protocols/how-to-authenticate-an-imap-pop-smtp-application-by-using-oauth 

https://me.sap.com/notes/3500322 : Error: javax.mail.AuthenticationFailedException when Sending Email via Office365 Smtp Server
Resolution
Currently, SAP Commerce Cloud does not support OAuth authentication for SMTP connections, so custom email sending mechanisms must be implemented to accommodate this requirement.

Regards,
Prasanth
SAP Support

entry.title: Strategies for Downtime-Optimized DMO : doDMO, doC, DMOVE2S4 for large databases
entry.content:
Context and Challenges:
Recently I got to migrate a very large non-HANA database of 50+ TB to HANA at AWS using Downtime-Optimized DMO (doDMO)
While this was a shift from on-premise to AWS , the customer sought to migrate on HANA at AWS first i.e. to validate data compression, operations at 32TB U7inh EC2 instance of AWS etc, and decided to change the Suite subsequently later. Thus, the plan for using DMO move to SAP S/4HANA (DMOVE2S4) approach along with Downtime-Optimized Conversion (doC) had to be dropped

Operating in R3load pipeline mode ,doDMO is much faster compared to the usual DMO with system move approach that operates at file mode only. However, since downtime-optimized options (doDMO and doC) do not support System Move, thus, in order to migrate only the database using doDMO , a customer confronts the challenges:
1. No Application migration : Customer has to install separate SAP application, and make necessary changes and connect the new SAP application at migrated database.
2. No usability of Source DB upon migration : Customer has to restore the Source database or scrap it as the database cannot be used for an SAP system, since DMO artifacts remain in it even upon Database migration is completed.

Do DMOVE2S4 and DMO with System Move solve these problems?
DMOVE2S4 is just an approach that can be incorporated with DMO, doDMO and doC based migrations. It is not a "Selectable" choice in the SUM UI ,but a method that combines DMO without letting SUM to know that this approach is actually used. With DMOVE2S4, there is no change in application-specific tasks such Simplification Item-Check and activities such as FIN Data Conversion and they are performed in uptime/downtime based on the chosen options i.e. Standard or Downtime-Optimised.
Unlike usual SUM DMO, DMOVE2S4 lets SUM operate on the application, installed in Target environment (which initially is connected on Source database).
With the completion of DMOVE2S4 based migration, ASCS is also moved to target environment where application finds itself already connected to new database of DMO.

While DMOVE2S4 approach tries to solve the first problem to some extent, it comes with its own technical and infrastructure requirements such as Latency < 20 ms & Bandwidth > 400 MBit/s, and limitation that target must be S/4HANA.
DMO with System move option was incorporated in DMO to solve both the above problems, but since it operates at file mode, it is not very fast and downtime friendly.
Even parallel mode (using rsync for files) of DMO with System move may not match downtime-optimised DMO or even the normal DMO which also uses memory pipes for database migration.
Thus, for a data-center migration of a large database & application aiming no S/4HANA,we still need to make some necessary compromises.

Available choices for a customer

With the introduction of doDMO and doC, along with DMOVE2S4 approach, customers running SAP ERP or S/4HANA have multiple paths to transition their ERP to S/4HANA on hyperscalers such as AWS and Azure.
To the journey of S/4HANA, multiple options focused on reducing the Conversion or migration-downtime brings us the situations to decide between the two questions : possible and optimal.
The diagram below demonstrates the optimal choice that is possible for the different cases of migrations, keeping an eye on business-downtime.
Factors such as cost and complexity can often be inversely proportional to the downtime-optimized by different ways, and they must be traded-off well.

HANA Decision Tree

 In case SUM DMO is not supported, we always have option for classical - export import of flat files for both homogeneous and heterogeneous migration using SWPM.

entry.title: Clean Core – Released API Alternative for Table TMP_SPEC
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on Clean Core / ABAP Cloud compliance in our S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise system and are addressing findings from ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) checks.

Background

During our analysis, we identified usage of the following standard table:

  • Table: TMP_SPEC
  • Application Component: MM-SRV (External Services Management)

This table is currently accessed directly in our custom code.

Business Requirement

We use table TMP_SPEC to:

  • Read model (template) service specifications
  • Retrieve service specification data required for maintenance planning and service processing

Current Challenge

  • Direct table access is flagged as non-compliant under Clean Core guidelines
  • We were unable to find:
    • Any released CDS View
    • Any released API / function module

that provides equivalent functionality for accessing template service specifications

Request

We would like to request clarification on the following:

  1. Released API Availability
    Is there any released API / CDS View / RAP BO available as an alternative to:
    • Read model/template service specifications
    • Access service specification data in a Clean Core-compliant way
  2. Recommended Approach
    What is the SAP-recommended Clean Core compliant approach to:
    • Work with service specification templates
    • Avoid direct access to table TMP_SPEC
  3. Future Roadmap
    Are there any plans to:
    • Release an API or CDS view for this functionality
    • Provide a successor object for TMP_SPEC

System Details

  • Product: S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise
  • Context: Clean Core transformation and ATC compliance

Objective

Our goal is to:

  • Eliminate direct table access (TMP_SPEC)
  • Use only released and upgrade-safe APIs
  • Ensure compliance with Clean Core principles

We appreciate your support in confirming whether a compliant alternative exists or if an Influence Request should be raised.

entry.title: New Multi-Availability Zone Option for SAP BTP ABAP Environment
entry.content:
Starting March 20, 2026, SAP BTP ABAP environment introduces the multi-availability zone (multi-AZ) option for ABAP environment instances, further strengthening system resilience.

When enabled, the ABAP runtime components of an SAP BTP ABAP environment instance are distributed across multiple availability zones within a region. In parallel, the SAP HANA Cloud database is configured in a high-availability setup with a primary and a secondary instance in different availability zones, using synchronous replication. This ensures that, in case of infrastructure failures, workloads can continue running with minimal disruption.

Compared to the default single availability zone setup, the multi-AZ option provides:

  • Distribution of ABAP application servers across availability zones
  • Higher prioritization during recovery scenarios
  • Automatic failover to a secondary database instance
  • Improved resilience in case of availability zone outages

It is important to note that the multi-availability zone option does not affect ABAP runtime costs (ACUs). However, persistence layer costs increase, as the SAP HANA Cloud database is deployed with an additional secondary instance (HCUs).

The option is configured at SAP BTP ABAP environment instance level and is particularly relevant for scenarios with higher availability and resilience requirements.

For more details, see Resilience of an ABAP Environment System.

entry.title: Accumulated Static Actuals in SAC Planning using Data Actions – Step-by-Step Solution
entry.content:
Requirement

In SAP Analytics Cloud Planning, we had a requirement to calculate accumulated (cumulative) actual values over time, but the values should remain static (not recalculated dynamically in stories).

Problem Statement

  • Need cumulative actuals (e.g., Jan → Jan, Feb → Jan+Feb, Mar → Jan+Feb+Mar).
  • Values should be stored in the model (not calculated on the fly).
  • Standard calculated measures were not suitable because they are dynamic.

Solution Approach

Hi Experts,

I would like to share a working solution to calculate Accumulated Actual (Life-To-Date) in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and publish it into the model using Data Action and Multi Action.

🔹 Data Action Script

CONFIG.TIME_HIERARCHY = CALENDARYEAR
CONFIG.GENERATE_UNBOOKED_DATA = OFF

MEMBERSET [d/Measures] = ("Actual", "Life_To_Date")
MEMBERSET [d/Date] = "201801" TO PERIOD(TODAY())

VARIABLEMEMBER #prevLTD OF [d/Measures]
VARIABLEMEMBER #thisActual OF [d/Measures]

FOREACH [d/Date]

    // Fetch previous month Life-To-Date
    DATA([d/Measures] = #prevLTD) =
        RESULTLOOKUP(
            [d/Measures] = "Life_To_Date",
            [d/Date] = PREVIOUS(1, "MONTH", [d/Date])
        )

    // Fetch current month Actual
    DATA([d/Measures] = #thisActual) =
        RESULTLOOKUP(
            [d/Measures] = "Actual",
            [d/Date] = [d/Date]
        )

    // Calculate cumulative value
    DATA([d/Measures] = "Life_To_Date") =
        RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = #prevLTD) +
        RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = #thisActual)

    // "Life_To_Date" holds the final output

ENDFOR

entry.title: Project Nexus: How I Wired a Local LLM to SAP S/4HANA and Automated AP Blocker Resolution
entry.content:
Every SAP Procure-to-Pay team I've worked with has the same problem. SAP Signavio Process Intelligence is excellent at detecting conformance violations — missing tax codes, price mismatches, posting blocks. But detection is only half the job. Between Signavio raising a flag and the invoice actually being resolved, there's a gap filled entirely by manual effort, error risk, and zero structured logging of why a decision was made.

I built Project Nexus to close that gap. Here's what it does, how it works, and what I learned.

What Nexus Does

Nexus is an autonomous P2P blocker resolution agent. The moment Signavio detects a violation, Nexus:

  • Receives the blocker event via webhook (under 1 second)
  • Retrieves the relevant policy — tax document or price agreement — using RAG against embedded PDFs
  • Reasons over the problem using a local 32B-parameter LLM (Qwen 2.5 Coder via Ollama — fully private, no cloud API)
  • Executes the fix in SAP via standard certified OData APIs — zero ABAP, zero core modifications
  • Logs every decision: AI confidence score, policy source, SAP response, timestamp
  • Routes high-value exceptions to a human approver with one-click Approve/Reject via email

End-to-end resolution time for routine cases: under 90 seconds.

 

Two Blocker Types in Scope

TAX_GAP — Missing Vendor Tax Jurisdiction Code

When a vendor is missing a TXJCD (tax jurisdiction code) in their Business Partner master data, SAP blocks invoice posting. Nexus detects this, queries the embedded tax policy PDF for the vendor's state and county, gets the correct SAP tax code from the LLM (confidence: 1.0 on all test cases), and POSTs it to API_BUSINESS_PARTNER. Done.

Real result: Vendor 17300001 (Acme Industrial Supplies, Indiana/Delaware County) had no TXJCD. Nexus applied tax code I1 (7%) in 43 seconds. Zero human involvement.

 

PRICE_VARIANCE — Invoice vs. PO Price Mismatch

SAP Error M8 081 fires when invoice price deviates from PO price beyond tolerance — triggering an MM-IV verification block in MRBR. Nexus retrieves the vendor's price agreement, calculates the variance, and makes a binary decision:

 

VarianceAction

≤ $500 (within tolerance)

Auto-release via Release FunctionImport on API_SUPPLIERINVOICE_PROCESS_SRV

> $500 (exceeds threshold)

HITL: context saved to PostgreSQL, approval email sent within 60 seconds

 

One SAP-specific lesson here: the MM-IV block cannot be released by PATCH on A_SupplierInvoice — those fields are non-updatable via OData. The correct mechanism is the Release Function. Once you know this, it's clean. It took a while to find it.

 

Human-in-the-Loop: Stateless by Design

The HITL pattern deserves its own mention because the naive approach — n8n Wait nodes — fails in production. A system restart kills all waiting executions. Nexus uses a stateless two-workflow pattern instead:

  • Main workflow saves all blocker context to PostgreSQL and terminates
  • Approver receives an HTML email with the full variance breakdown and direct Approve/Reject webhook links
  • Clicking a link fires a separate HITL Decision workflow, which reads context from Postgres, calls SAP, and logs the outcome
  • Idempotency guard: clicking the link twice does nothing twice — status check prevents duplicate SAP writes

The result: concurrent HITL items, resilience to restarts, and a clean audit trail whether the approver clicks Approve, Reject, or nothing at all.

 

The Architecture in One Paragraph

Clean Core Sidecar pattern. n8n orchestrates everything. Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B runs locally on an RTX 5070 via Ollama — vendor data never leaves the network. Policy documents (tax policy PDF, price agreements PDF) are chunked, embedded with nomic-embed-text, and stored in PostgreSQL with pgvector. Every resolution writes to five PostgreSQL audit tables and appends a row to a six-tab Google Sheets dashboard. SAP is touched only through standard OData.

 

BTP Tomorrow.

Every component was chosen with the production architecture in mind. The swap is straightforward:

POC (n8n + Local)Production (SAP BTP)

Qwen 2.5 Coder via Ollama

SAP AI Core — Generative AI Hub

pgvector embeddings

SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine

n8n HTTP nodes

SAP Integration Suite iFlows (OData adapter)

PostgreSQL audit tables

SAP HANA Cloud + BTP Audit Log Service

Tailscale connectivity

SAP Integration Suite Cloud Connector

 

Same logic. Same APIs. Same Clean Core principle. Only the runtime changes.

 

What This Proves

AI agents for SAP automation are not a future concept. They are working today, on standard APIs, with production-grade auditability, and without touching the SAP core. The technology stack — process intelligence, local LLMs, RAG, OData — is mature enough to build on right now.

The Action Gap between detection and resolution is closable. Project Nexus closes it in under 90 seconds.

 

Signavio identifies the gap. Nexus closes it. Humans decide when it counts.

entry.title: How LLMs handle unrelated statements
entry.content:
LLMs do their best to respond to buggy confusing prompts.

 

User prompt

I hesitate to go to see Falstaff.

Arizona is full of snakes and cactuses, isn’t it?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

I think there might be some confusion here! "Falstaff" is an opera by Giuseppe Verdi (or a Shakespeare character), not a place in Arizona.

Are you perhaps thinking of:

  • Visiting Arizona and worried about snakes and cacti?
  • Seeing the opera/play Falstaff at a theater in Arizona?

To address your actual concerns: Yes, Arizona does have snakes and cacti, but they're generally not a problem for tourists visiting cities or attending performances. If you're going to see Falstaff at an opera house or theater in a city like Phoenix or Tucson, you'd be indoors and perfectly safe!

What are you actually planning to do?

 

Answer from Perplexity Sonar

Your query contains two unrelated statements. I can address the first part about seeing Falstaff, but the second statement about Arizona appears disconnected from your main question.

Regarding Falstaff: If you're hesitant about attending a performance, it may help to know that the work is a madcap comedy designed to entertain.[5] The plot follows Sir John Falstaff, who attempts to seduce two wealthy married women, Alice Ford and Meg Page, to access their husbands' fortunes.[5] However, the women discover his scheme and, with help from their husbands and friends, orchestrate increasingly humorous tricks against him—including throwing him into the River Thames and dressing him in disguise.[1][2]

The story culminates in a final prank in the woods where Falstaff is tormented by people dressed as supernatural creatures.[1] By the end, Falstaff accepts his comeuppance with good humor, and the play concludes happily for everyone involved.[1]

If you enjoy comedies with clever characters, mistaken identities, and physical humor, Falstaff (whether as Shakespeare's play or Verdi's opera adaptation) offers lighthearted entertainment. If you have specific concerns about the performance—such as length, language, or content—those might be worth researching for the particular production you're considering.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite

It sounds like you're weighing a few things! Let's break down your hesitations:

Hesitation about seeing Falstaff:

Could you tell me a bit more about why you're hesitating to see Falstaff? Knowing your reasons will help me offer more specific advice. For example, are you:

  • Unfamiliar with the opera? If so, I can tell you about the plot, the music, or suggest resources to learn more.
  • Worried it might be too long or difficult? Falstaff is known for its comedic brilliance and its relatively compact duration for an opera, but I can address any concerns you have.
  • Not sure if it's your kind of humor? I can describe the type of comedy it is.
  • Concerned about the production itself? If you know who is performing or directing, that might be a factor.

Arizona's reputation:

You're absolutely right, Arizona is indeed famous for its snakes and cactuses! These are iconic elements of the desert landscape.

  • Cactuses: Arizona is a haven for a huge variety of cacti, from the towering Saguaro to smaller, more prickly varieties. They are a defining feature of the state's natural beauty.
  • Snakes: Yes, Arizona does have snakes, including some venomous ones like rattlesnakes. It's wise to be aware of this if you're exploring the outdoors, especially in more remote or natural areas. However, most of the time, encounters are rare, and with common sense precautions, it's not something to be overly anxious about for most visitors.

Connecting the two:

Is there a connection you're making between your hesitation to see Falstaff and your thoughts about Arizona? For example, are you feeling a bit hesitant about venturing into something new or potentially "uncomfortable" (like the idea of snakes for some people), and Falstaff feels like that to you?

Once I understand your hesitations better, I can offer more tailored thoughts!

 

Answer from GPT-5 Mini

If you mean to check grammar and naturalness, these sound fine but can be improved:

  • "I hesitate to go to see Falstaff."
    More natural: "I'm hesitant to go see Falstaff." or "I'm hesitant to go and see Falstaff."
    Explanation: "hesitate" in simple present is okay, but "I'm hesitant to..." or "I hesitate to..." followed by the bare infinitive "go see" is more idiomatic than "go to see."
  • "Arizona is full of snakes and cactuses, isn't it?"
    More natural: "Arizona is full of snakes and cacti, isn't it?"
    Explanation: Both "cactuses" and "cacti" are correct; "cacti" is more common. The tag question "isn't it?" is fine after that statement.

Do you want alternatives with different tone (formal, casual) or were you asking about factual accuracy (how many snakes/cacti are actually in Arizona)?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

It looks like your message contains two unrelated thoughts. Could you clarify what you're looking for?

  1. Falstaff - Are you referring to the opera by Verdi, a Shakespeare play, or something else? I'd be happy to help you decide whether it might be worth seeing.
  2. Arizona - Yes, Arizona is known for its desert landscape, including various cacti (like the famous saguaro) and snakes (including rattlesnakes). Is there something specific you'd like to know about Arizona?

Feel free to let me know how I can help with either topic!

 

Answer from Gemini Pro

Ah, I see the connection you're making! It sounds like you're considering a trip to see Verdi's opera Falstaff at the Arizona Opera.

You've perfectly combined the cultural event with the local stereotype!

To address your points:

  1. Falstaff is a brilliant, joyful, and hilarious opera. It was Verdi's final masterpiece, and it's a wonderful experience. If you enjoy comedy and fantastic music, you will likely love the performance.
  2. Arizona, snakes, and cactuses: You're not wrong that the Sonoran Desert is home to many species of cactus and snake. However, if you're traveling to Phoenix or Tucson for the opera, you will be in a large, modern city. Your chances of an unwelcome encounter with a snake are extremely low unless you go hiking deep in the desert and turn over rocks.

You'll definitely see beautiful cactuses (especially the giant saguaros), but they are easy to avoid touching.

Think of it this way: You're more likely to be bothered by traffic on the way to the opera house than by a rattlesnake.

So, you should hesitate if you don't like comedy or opera, but the local wildlife shouldn't be a deterrent. It sounds like a wonderful trip—the combination of a brilliant opera and a unique, beautiful landscape is hard to beat

 

I was hoping to be asked: “Do you mean Flagstaff, the city in Arizona?”.

entry.title: Variable inheritance issue between classic and optimized stories when opening via links, hyperlinks
entry.content:
Problem Description
In the source story, there is a KPI table with links to other stories.
The behavior when opening these links differs depending on:

The type of target story (classic vs optimized)
Whether the link opens in the same tab or a new tab
The target story configuration (saved or server values)

 

1. Classic Story

When opening the link, the selected variables (Mes/Año and Programa) are correctly inherited in the target story.
The variable prompt appears pre-filled with values from the source story.

2. Optimized Story

If the target story is configured to use server-defined values, it does not inherit the current variables.
If configured to use current variable selections, it works correctly.
Even when adding parameters in the URL (vXXPar, vXXVal or fXXModel, fXXDim, fXXVal), the variable prompt remains empty.

3. Opening Method

Script onClick with NavigationUtils.openStory:
Same tab: sometimes variables are inherited
New tab: variable prompt is empty.
Hyperlink in table or widget:
Same tab: similar behavior to script.
New tab: variable prompt is empty, values not inherited.

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: SAP Note 2743317 Does not work
entry.content:
SAP Note 2743317 provides guidance for missing authorizations, but it doesn’t cover every backend scenario. The object QFGEN is tied to Quality Management (QM) Fiori apps and requires specific authorization objects (e.g., S_SERVICE, S_RFC, S_USER_GRP). If your system has custom roles or has not been updated with the latest support package, the note alone won’t resolve the issue.

Having said that, the recommended next actions are

  • Run SU53 immediately after the error to pinpoint missing authorizations.
  • Compare your user’s role with the SAP standard business role for the app.
  • If still unresolved, raise an OSS message to SAP referencing Note 2743317 and include your SU53 trace.

3. Is there any known limitations or drawbacks?


--- Support Assistant ---

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI UPGRADE
entry.content:
Hello customer,

Thank you for your question.

SAP always recommend to upgrade the SAP GUI to the latest available patch level in:

The latest SAP GUI version and patch can be downloaded from the SAP for Me Support Portal:

Software Center - SAP for Me

# "Installations & Upgrades"

 # "By Category"

  # "SAP Frontend Components"

   # "SAP GUI FOR WINDOWS"

    # "SAP GUI FOR WINDOWS 8.00 CORE "

     # "Support Packages and Patches"

        # SAP GUI for Windows 8.00 PL16 (Note 1053737) ----> always check this note for updates regarding patch releases.

 

The important changes and improvements in SAP GUI 8.00 are outlined in the SAP Note 3075781 - New and changed features in SAP GUI for Windows 8.00.

The most relevant change and improvements refers to the 64bit version availability: 

True 64bit version of SAP GUI for Windows

Up to release 7.70, SAP GUI for Windows was only available as a 32bit version with the exception being the 64bit versions of the automation controls (Logon Control, Table Factory Control, BAPI Control and Function Control). The 32bit version works well on 64bit operating systems and runs in the so-called “emulation” (see also https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/winprog64/running-32-bit-applications). However, this has a couple of drawbacks when it comes to memory utilization and integration with other 64bit processes. The most important limitation in this area was overcome when we started supporting 64bit Microsoft Office versions in SAP Desktop Office Integration.

Regardless of this, we keep getting requests for full 64bit support in SAP GUI for Windows and thus decided to implement, deliver, and support such a version. Therefore, as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.00, the SAP GUI delivery includes both a 32bit version and a 64bit version. You can decide which version to use (parallel usage of both versions on the same client PC is not possible).
The 64bit version offers an almost complete feature set with some differences to the 32bit version which are documented in SAP Note 3218166 - "SAP GUI for Windows: Functional differences of the 64bit version compared to the 32bit version".

Although there are some restrictions with 64bit version, in case those restrictions outlined SAP Note 3218166 are not relevant within your SAP landscape scenario, we  recommend to upgrade to this bit version due to memory utilization and compatibility with other 64-bit external softwares. 

 

Best Regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Issue with Location-Level Restriction Not Working
entry.content:
Run STAUTHTRACE for the affected user while they post in MIGO. If you are not the part of Security team, ask them to execute this transaction.  This will show if the system is checking M_MSEG_LGO or any other storage location–related objects. If the check is not happening, it means the restriction is not active in your Public Cloud environment.

Check Business Role Restrictions in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Unlike on-premise, Public Cloud uses business role restrictions (not just authorization objects). Navigate to Maintain Business Roles → Restrictions → Storage Location and configure allowed values. Block Storage Location in Customizing (if available in your edition). Some Public Cloud tenants allow blocking storage locations for postings. This ensures no entries can be made, regardless of role.

entry.title: Re: ZERO base price requirement in sales order
entry.content: For descriptive repair items, use TATX text items directly in the sales order. For materials that must exist in master data, assign SERV material type and maintain PPR0 with 0 value. If neither works, raise an SAP support ticket to confirm if a fit-to-standard workaround exists in Public Cloud for repair processes.

entry.title: Digital signature in Adobe Forms
entry.content:
Hello, all.

I am trying to use digital signatures with Adobe Forms.

I have created an Adobe Form that works correctly and is generated/printed without any issues.

I have now added a new Digital Signature field, and it is located in the following position in the hierarchy:

This is the code I'm using after the FP_JOB_CLOSE:

    DATA: lo_fp     TYPE REF TO if_fp,
          lo_pdfobj TYPE REF TO if_fp_pdf_object,
          lv_signed TYPE xstring,
          lv_alias  TYPE string,
          lv_field  TYPE string.

    lv_field = 'data.pagina_principal.Firma_Digital'.
*    lv_field = 'data.Firma_Digital[0]'.

    TRY.
        lo_fp     = cl_fp=>get_reference( ).
        lo_pdfobj = lo_fp->create_pdf_object(
                      connection = 'ADS_SSL' ). "ls_fp_outputparams-connection ). "cl_fp=>get_ads_connection( )

*lo_pdfobj->get_pdf_fields(

***      Test
**        lo_pdfobj->set_template(

**          fillable = abap_true ).

        " Indicar alias y campo
        lo_pdfobj->set_signature(
          keyname   = lv_alias
          fieldname = lv_field ).

        " Ejecutar la firma
        lo_pdfobj->execute( ).

        " Recuperar PDF firmado
        lo_pdfobj->get_document( IMPORTING pdfdata = lv_signed ).

        ev_pdf = lv_signed.

      CATCH cx_fp_runtime_usage INTO DATA(lx_usage).
        ev_mensaje = lx_usage->get_longtext( ).
        IF ev_mensaje IS INITIAL.
          ev_mensaje = lx_usage->errmsgshort.
        ENDIF.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 3 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

From reactive triage to proactive monitoring — a multi-agent system with a router that delegates SM21 system log errors and SM37 job failures to specialized sub-agents, correlates findings across log types, and escalates autonomously.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 3 introduces the multi-agent pattern — a router agent that classifies incoming SAP errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents: one for SM21 system log analysis, one for SM37 job failure triage. The router also identifies correlations across log types that neither sub-agent sees alone. The article covers two stages: Stage A reactive triage (triggered on demand) and Stage B proactive monitoring (continuous, schedule-driven). This is the architectural foundation for the multi-agent orchestration in Part 6.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Parts 1 and 2 built single agents — a data quality assessor and a migration assessment agent with conditional routing. Part 3 introduces multi-agent coordination: a router agent that classifies errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents. This pattern is the core building block for Part 6, where all agents in this series work together under a unified orchestrator.

The exception log triage use case is ideal for introducing multi-agent patterns because SAP error landscapes involve genuinely different log types that require different analysis approaches. A single agent handling SM21 and SM37 in the same reasoning chain produces lower quality findings than two specialized agents, each focused on their domain, coordinated by a router that sees the full picture.

WHY MULTI-AGENT FOR SAP LOG TRIAGE 

Why Log Triage Benefits from Multi-Agent Architecture

SAP exception triage in production environments involves multiple log sources that are related but structurally different:

Log Type

What It Contains

Who Cares

SM21 System Log

Platform-level errors — work process failures, memory issues, RFC connection problems, kernel errors. Context is technical infrastructure.

SAP Basis / Platform team

SM37 Job Log

Business process failures — scheduled job errors, batch processing failures, data load problems. Context is business process continuity.

Functional / Operations team

Correlation layer

When an SM37 job fails because of an SM21 RFC connection error — neither log alone tells the full story. The router sees both.

Both teams need joint view

A single agent reasoning over both log types simultaneously tends to conflate the contexts — applying infrastructure reasoning to business process errors and vice versa. Specialized sub-agents with a coordinating router produce more accurate findings and clearer escalation paths.

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Router + Sub-Agent Architecture

Error batch input → Router agent classifies → SM21 sub-agent → Correlate findings → Severity score + escalation
		→	SM37 sub-agent	→

The router agent has three responsibilities:

  • Classify incoming errors — determine which log type each error belongs to
  • Delegate to the appropriate specialized sub-agent
  • Correlate findings across both sub-agents — identify cases where SM21 infrastructure errors are causing SM37 job failures

 

Agent

Responsibility

Router agent

Classifies errors, delegates to sub-agents, correlates cross-log findings, produces unified severity score

SM21 sub-agent

Analyzes system log errors — identifies platform issues, RFC failures, memory problems, kernel errors

SM37 sub-agent

Analyzes job log failures — identifies which jobs failed, why, business process impact, retry feasibility

STATE SCHEMA AND TOOLS 

State Schema and Tool Definitions

Triage State Schema
# triage_agent.py — multi-agent exception log triage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class TriageState(TypedDict):
    raw_errors:         List[dict]   # Incoming error batch
    sm21_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM21 errors
    sm37_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM37 errors
    sm21_findings:      List[dict]   # SM21 sub-agent findings
    sm37_findings:      List[dict]   # SM37 sub-agent findings
    correlations:       List[dict]   # Cross-log correlations
    overall_severity:   str          # CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
    escalation_needed:  bool
    escalation_targets: List[str]    # Which teams to notify
    triage_report:      Optional[str]

Tool Definitions

# tools/triage_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def get_sm21_errors(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM21 system log errors from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            MANDT, DATUM, UZEIT, MTYPE,
            MSGID, MSGNO, MSGTX,
            REPID, ABAP_RUNTIME_ERROR
        FROM SYSLOG
        WHERE DATUM >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        AND MTYPE IN ('E', 'A', 'X')
        -- E=Error, A=Abend, X=Exit
        ORDER BY DATUM DESC, UZEIT DESC
        LIMIT 200
    ''')
    errors = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        errors.append({
            'date': str(row[1]), 'time': str(row[2]),
            'type': row[3], 'message_id': row[4],
            'message_text': row[6], 'program': row[7],
            'runtime_error': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm21_errors': errors, 'count': len(errors)})
 
@tool
def get_sm37_failures(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM37 job failures from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            JOBNAME, JOBCOUNT, STATUS,
            SDLSTRTDT, SDLSTRTTM,
            LASTCHGDT, LASTCHGTM,
            JOBCLASS, PRGNAME
        FROM TBTCO
        WHERE STATUS = 'A'
        -- A = Aborted
        AND SDLSTRTDT >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        ORDER BY LASTCHGDT DESC, LASTCHGTM DESC
        LIMIT 100
    ''')
    failures = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        failures.append({
            'job_name': row[0], 'job_count': row[1],
            'status': row[2], 'start_date': str(row[3]),
            'start_time': str(row[4]), 'job_class': row[7],
            'program': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm37_failures': failures, 'count': len(failures)})
 
@tool
def get_job_log_details(job_name: str, job_count: str) -> str:
    """Get detailed log messages for a specific failed SM37 job."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT MSGTY, MSGID, MSGNO, MSGV1, MSGV2
        FROM TBTCP
        WHERE JOBNAME = '{job_name}'
        AND JOBCOUNT = '{job_count}'
        AND MSGTY IN ('E', 'A')
        ORDER BY LNSNR
    ''')
    messages = [{'type': r[0], 'msg_id': r[1],
                 'msg_no': r[2], 'text': f'{r[3]} {r[4]}'.strip()}
                for r in cursor.fetchall()]
    return json.dumps({'job': job_name, 'messages': messages})
STAGE A: REACTIVE MULTI-AGENT TRIAGE 

Stage A — Reactive Triage on Demand

Stage A is triggered manually or by an integration call — it receives a batch of errors, classifies them, runs sub-agents, and produces a triage report. This is the right pattern for on-call support workflows and integration with ITSM tools like ServiceNow.

Router Agent

# Router: classifies errors and delegates to sub-agents
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
router_prompt = '''You are a SAP exception log router agent.
Your job is to:
1. Fetch recent SM21 system log errors using get_sm21_errors
2. Fetch recent SM37 job failures using get_sm37_failures
3. Count and categorize errors by type and severity
4. Identify the top 3 most critical issues requiring immediate attention
5. Note any patterns suggesting SM21 errors are causing SM37 failures
6. Produce a structured classification summary for sub-agent processing

Be systematic. Fetch both log types before drawing conclusions.
Always explain your classification reasoning.'''
 
router_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors, get_sm37_failures],
    state_modifier=router_prompt
)

SM21 Sub-Agent

# SM21 specialist: deep analysis of system log errors
sm21_prompt = '''You are an SAP Basis expert specializing in SM21 system logs.
Analyze the SM21 errors provided and for each significant error:

1. Identify the root cause (infrastructure, memory, RFC, kernel)
2. Assess business impact — which processes are affected
3. Classify severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
4. Recommend immediate action
5. Recommend preventive measure

Focus on patterns — a single error may be noise,
ten of the same error in one hour is a systemic issue.
Always reference specific error message IDs in your findings.'''
 
sm21_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors],
    state_modifier=sm21_prompt
)

SM37 Sub-Agent

# SM37 specialist: deep analysis of job failures
sm37_prompt = '''You are an SAP operations expert specializing in SM37 job failures.
For each failed job:

1. Use get_job_log_details to retrieve the specific failure messages
2. Identify the root cause (data issue, RFC failure, timeout, authorization)
3. Assess business process impact — what does this job do?
4. Classify severity based on job class and business criticality
5. Determine if the job can be safely restarted or requires fix-first
6. Identify dependent jobs that may be blocked

Job class A = highest priority. Always investigate class A failures first.'''
 
sm37_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm37_failures, get_job_log_details],
    state_modifier=sm37_prompt
)
Stage A Orchestration — Router + Sub-Agents
# Stage A: Orchestrate router + sub-agents reactively
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json
 
def run_reactive_triage(hours_back: int = 24) -> dict:
    """Run full reactive triage across SM21 and SM37 logs."""
 
    # Step 1: Router classifies and summarizes all errors
    router_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Fetch and classify all SAP errors from the last {hours_back} hours.
            Summarize SM21 system errors and SM37 job failures separately.
            Flag any cases where system errors appear to be causing job failures.
        ''')]
    })
    router_summary = router_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 2: SM21 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm21_result = sm21_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM21 system log errors from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm21_findings = sm21_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 3: SM37 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm37_result = sm37_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM37 job failures from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Get job log details for
            class A failures first. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm37_findings = sm37_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 4: Router correlates findings from both sub-agents
    correlation_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Correlate these findings from both log types and produce
            a unified triage report with:
            1. Overall severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
            2. Top 3 issues requiring immediate action
            3. Cross-log correlations (SM21 errors causing SM37 failures)
            4. Escalation targets (Basis team / Operations team / Both)
            5. Recommended actions in priority order

            SM21 findings: {sm21_findings}
            SM37 findings: {sm37_findings}
        ''')]
    })
 
    return {
        'router_summary': router_summary,
        'sm21_findings': sm21_findings,
        'sm37_findings': sm37_findings,
        'triage_report': correlation_result['messages'][-1].content
    }
 
# Run reactive triage
result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
print(result['triage_report'])

Passing the router summary as context to each sub-agent is the key pattern here. The sub-agents don't operate in isolation — they receive the router's classification as context, which focuses their analysis on the most significant errors rather than treating every log entry with equal weight.

STAGE B: PROACTIVE MONITORING AGENT 

Stage B — Proactive Monitoring with Scheduled Execution

Stage B wraps the Stage A reactive triage in a monitoring loop. The agent runs on a schedule, compares current error patterns against a baseline, and triggers triage automatically when anomalies are detected — without waiting for a human to initiate the process.

Scheduler triggers → Fetch current errors → Compare to baseline	→ Anomaly detected? → Run reactive triage	→ Escalate if needed

Baseline and Anomaly Detection

# monitoring.py — proactive monitoring wrapper
import json, time, os
from datetime import datetime
 
def get_error_baseline() -> dict:
    """Load stored baseline error counts for comparison."""
    # In production: store baseline in HANA or BTP configuration
    return {
        'sm21_hourly_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM21_BASELINE', '10')),
        'sm37_daily_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM37_BASELINE', '5')),
        'anomaly_threshold': 2.0
        # Trigger if errors > 2x baseline
    }
 
def check_for_anomalies(current_counts: dict, baseline: dict) -> dict:
    """Compare current error counts against baseline."""
    sm21_ratio = current_counts.get('sm21_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm21_hourly_avg'], 1)
    sm37_ratio = current_counts.get('sm37_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm37_daily_avg'], 1)
    threshold = baseline['anomaly_threshold']
 
    return {
        'anomaly_detected': sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold,
        'sm21_ratio': round(sm21_ratio, 2),
        'sm37_ratio': round(sm37_ratio, 2),
        'trigger_reason': (
            f'SM21 errors at {sm21_ratio:.1f}x baseline' if sm21_ratio > threshold
            else f'SM37 failures at {sm37_ratio:.1f}x baseline'
        ) if sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold else None
    }
 
def send_escalation(triage_report: str, targets: list):
    """Send escalation via Microsoft Graph API or Teams webhook."""
    # Integration with MS Graph covered in the training automation article
    # For BTP CF deployment: use requests to call Graph API
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Escalation sent to: {targets}')
    print(triage_report[:500])
    # Truncated for logging
 
def run_monitoring_cycle():
    """Single monitoring cycle — run on schedule via CF Scheduler."""
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Starting monitoring cycle...')
 
    # Quick error count check — lightweight, runs every cycle
    sm21_data = json.loads(get_sm21_errors.invoke({'hours_back': 1}))
    sm37_data = json.loads(get_sm37_failures.invoke({'hours_back': 24}))
 
    current_counts = {
        'sm21_count': sm21_data.get('count', 0),
        'sm37_count': sm37_data.get('count', 0)
    }
 
    baseline = get_error_baseline()
    anomaly = check_for_anomalies(current_counts, baseline)
 
    if anomaly['anomaly_detected']:
        print(f'Anomaly detected: {anomaly["trigger_reason"]}')
        # Run full reactive triage
        result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=4)
        # Triage last 4 hours on anomaly
 
        # Escalate if triage confirms high severity
        if 'CRITICAL' in result['triage_report'] or 'HIGH' in result['triage_report']:
            send_escalation(
                result['triage_report'],
                targets=['basis-team@company.com', 'operations@company.com']
            )
    else:
        print(f'No anomaly. SM21: {current_counts["sm21_count"]} errors,
              SM37: {current_counts["sm37_count"]} failures — within baseline.')
 
# BTP CF Flask entry point — triggered by CF Scheduler
from flask import Flask, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
 
@app.route('/monitor', methods=['POST'])
def monitor():
    run_monitoring_cycle()
    return jsonify({'status': 'ok', 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat()})
 
@app.route('/triage', methods=['POST'])
def triage():
    # Manual reactive triage endpoint
    result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
    return jsonify(result)

The baseline values (SM21_BASELINE, SM37_BASELINE) are set as BTP environment variables in your manifest.yml. Start with conservative baselines — set them to your current average error counts — and tighten them over time as you observe the agent's sensitivity. Anomaly thresholds that are too low produce alert fatigue. Start at 2x baseline and adjust based on false positive rate.

STAGE A VS STAGE B 

When to Use Each Pattern

Consideration

Stage A — Reactive

Stage B — Proactive

Trigger

Manual or integration call

Scheduled — runs every N minutes

Use case

On-call support, ITSM integration, ad-hoc investigation

Production monitoring, overnight batch, continuous operations

Cost

Runs only when called — lower LLM cost

Runs on schedule — baseline check is lightweight, full triage only on anomaly

Deployment

REST endpoint on BTP CF

CF Scheduler + REST endpoint on BTP CF

Recommended for

Support teams, incident response workflows

Operations teams, 24x7 production environments

NEW PATTERN: ROUTER + SUB-AGENT COORDINATION 

What This Part Introduces for Part 6

Part 3 introduces the router + sub-agent coordination pattern that Part 6 uses to orchestrate all four series agents together. The key principles that carry forward:

  • The router has a global view — it sees all error types and identifies cross-domain correlations that sub-agents cannot see working independently
  • Sub-agents are specialists — they receive focused context from the router and produce higher quality findings than a generalist agent would
  • Context passing is explicit — each sub-agent receives the router's summary as input context, not just raw data
  • Correlation is the router's unique value — the pattern where SM21 infrastructure errors cause SM37 job failures is only visible to the agent with the cross-log view

In Part 6, this same router pattern becomes the orchestrator that delegates to the migration assessment agent from Part 2, the master data quality agent from Part 4, and the S/4HANA readiness agent from Part 5. The triage agent from this part also participates as a sub-agent when the orchestrator detects operational anomalies during assessment runs.

What's Next — Part 4

  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains (material, vendor, customer), generates a scored remediation plan, and integrates with the HANA vector store RAG pipeline from Series 1 for context-enriched findings.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends the Part 2 migration assessment with clean core analysis, deprecated function module and BAPI detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping for upgrade impact scoring.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents coordinated by a unified orchestrator that delegates, synthesizes, and produces a complete SAP landscape intelligence report.

The router + sub-agent pattern from this article is reusable across any SAP domain with multiple related data sources. The same architecture applies to financial audit log analysis, security violation detection across multiple authorization logs, and performance monitoring across work process and database logs.

entry.title: #SITBH2026 🎤 S/4HANA Finance na Prática
entry.content:
Bio: @Marssel700 Marssel Vilaça é instrutor e consultor SAP com atuação em projetos de implementação e transformação digital utilizando SAP S/4HANA. Possui experiência prática no mercado e se dedica à disseminação de conhecimento técnico por meio de conteúdos educacionais e participação ativa na comunidade SAP.

Descrição da Palestra

A palestra apresentada no SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026 #SITBH2026 aborda experiências reais em projetos SAP S/4HANA, com foco nos principais aprendizados adquiridos durante implementações. O conteúdo traz uma visão prática do que acontece nos bastidores dos projetos, destacando desafios, decisões estratégicas e pontos de atenção para profissionais da área

Resumo do Conteúdo

A apresentação inicia com uma introdução ao contexto do evento SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026, destacando a importância da comunidade SAP e dos encontros presenciais para troca de conhecimento.

O palestrante contextualiza sua atuação como consultor e instrutor, explicando que o objetivo da palestra é compartilhar experiências reais de projetos com SAP S/4HANA, especialmente para aqueles que não puderam acompanhar o evento presencialmente.

Entre os principais pontos abordados:

A relevância do SAP S/4HANA como plataforma central para transformação digital nas empresas.
A importância de compreender o cenário real dos projetos, indo além da teoria e das documentações oficiais.
A necessidade de preparação adequada dos profissionais SAP para lidar com desafios práticos durante implementações.
O valor da troca de experiências dentro da comunidade, permitindo aprendizado contínuo e evolução profissional.
A apresentação foi originalmente realizada no ambiente do Localiza Labs, reforçando a conexão entre empresas inovadoras e a comunidade SAP.

O conteúdo também enfatiza que cada projeto possui particularidades, exigindo flexibilidade, conhecimento técnico e visão estratégica dos consultores envolvidos.

Considerações Finais

A palestra reforça a importância de compartilhar experiências reais como forma de acelerar o aprendizado coletivo na comunidade SAP. Eventos como o SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026 se mostram essenciais para conectar profissionais, disseminar conhecimento prático e preparar o mercado para os desafios das implementações com SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: Re: Transaction Notification to Block creating vendor
entry.content:
Hi @Kishorathippatta ,

Starting from SAP Business One version 10.0 FP 2108, you can add user_id (integer type) as the 6th parameter into the procedure SBO_SP_TransactionNotification.

Refer to SAP Note 1320484 for more details.

Kind regards,

ANKIT CHAUHAN

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: What makes an employee-churn model usable in the real world?
entry.content:
Just to add from a governance slant, we have heard from customer that embedding governance in systems that are not designed for it is hard!  On the topic of trust, ladies and gentlemen, promote the idea of a data quality analyst, data owner, or data steward for your key data domains - that way you can do your job faster, because you can trust the data! Some thoughts from SAP and non-SAP customers through some recent research:

  • Data environments are often built quickly for analytics and reporting
  • Ownership, documentation, governance controls are underdeveloped; accountability gaps emerge
  • Trust Owners must retrofit governance into fragmented ecosystems with inconsistent definitions/unclear lineage

There is a better way - and so much is automated, but with the human touch and true feels for the business.

Engineers and developers, CTA:  get your data governance started with the people that know the business processes/analytics and the data itself to own that data.  I believe that then you can do your job way more efficiently - and more importantly trust your outcomes. My two cents 😊
